- `description`:

  ```
  Patience is the inner quality of continuing to wait and trust without being unravelled by delay or difficulty. The Hebrew vocabulary here includes silence, the stilling of inner turmoil in the face of what is hard; waiting in the sense of active, expectant trust; and the weariness that must be borne without giving up. The Greek adds the sense of holding back under pressure. Patience is not passive but active: it is the continuous choice to keep trusting while the situation remains unresolved. It is counted among the fruits of the Spirit and the character of God.
  ```

- `lsj_entry`:

  ```
  ὑπομένω, 
  future -μενῶ conjecture in [Refs 4th c.BC+]:— stay behind, [Refs 8th c.BC+]; ὑπομεινον ἕως ἂν παραγένηται [Refs 3rd c.BC+]: also, remain alive, [Refs 5th c.BC+]: of things, to be left behind, remain, ὑπέμεινε τὸ παχύτερον [Refs 2nd c.AD+]: generally, to be permanent, [Refs 4th c.BC+]
  __II transitive, 
  __II.1 with accusative person, abide or await another, διὰ τοῦτό σε οὐχ ὑπέμενον [Refs 5th c.BC+]; especially await his attack, bide the onset, [Refs 8th c.BC+]; ὑ. τὰς Σειρῆνας abide their presence, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; of evils, κακῶν ὅσα ἡμᾶς ἐν ὑστέρῳ χρόνῳ ὑ. [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __II.2 with accusative of things, to be patient under, abide patiently, submit to any evil that threatens one, δουλείαν [Refs 5th c.BC+]; face, τὴν μέλλουσαν δουληΐην [Refs 5th c.BC+]; face up to, λόγον [Refs 5th c.BC+]; οὐχὑπέμειναν τὰς δωρεάς they could not abide the gifts, i. e. scorned to accept them, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; ὑ. τὴν κρίσιν await one's trial, [Refs 5th c.BC+]: generally, wait for, τὴν ἑορτήν [Refs 5th c.BC+]; μακρὸν οὐχ ὑπέμεινεν ὄλβον could not endure his great bliss, i. e. it turned his head, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __II.3 absolutely, stand one's ground, stand firm, [Refs 8th c.BC+]; ὑπομένων καρτερεῖν endure patiently, [Refs]
  __II.4 with infinitive, submit, bear, or dare to do a thing, wait to do, οὐδ᾽ ὑπέμεινε γνώμεναι he did not wait for us to know him, [Refs 8th c.BC+]; ὑ. πονεῖν he submitted to toil, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __II.5 with participle relating to the subject, εἰ ὑπομενέουσι χεῖρας ἐμοὶ ἀνταειρόμενοι if they shall dare to lift hand against me, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; ὑπομένεις με κηδεύων you persist in.., [Refs 5th c.BC+]; οὐχ ὑπομένει ὠφελούμενος he submits not to be helped, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __II.6 with participle relating to the object, ὑ. Ξέρξην ἐπιόντα await his coming, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; οὐ.. γὰρ ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ χωριζόμενον τὸ βρέφος ὑπέμενεν (i.e. τὸ θηρίον) it (the elephant) could not bear the infant's being removed, [Refs 3rd c.BC+]participle, φιλοῦντος ὑ. submit to his kissing, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]
  __II.7 in [false reading for{ἐπιμεμενηκώς}. ">Refs 2nd c.AD+]
  __II.8 promise, with future infinitive, [Refs 4th c.AD+]
  __II.9 admit of, like{δέχομαι}[Refs 1st c.BC+]
  __II.10 τὴν ναυτίαν οὐχ ὑπομένουσιν do not suffer from seasickness, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]; ἀλλοκότους φαντασίας τῆς ψυχῆς ὑπομενούσης experiencing, [Refs]; ὅταν ἔμφραξιν ὑπομένῃ ὁ πόρος χωρὶς αἰτίας undergoes obstruction, [Refs 6th c.AD+]

- `lsj_entry`:

  ```
  ἅπαξ [ᾰπ], 
  adverb once, once only, once for all, first in [Refs 8th c.BC+]; ἅ... ἀπὸ θυμὸν ὀλέσσαι[Refs 5th c.BC+]; οὐχ ἅ. μόνον more than once, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; of the self-creation of Νοῦς, τὴν ποίησιν αὑτοῦ.. ἅ. εἶναι[Refs 3rd c.AD+]; ἅ. ἔτι yet this once, [Refs 4th c.BC+]; τὸ ἅ. τοῦτο at this moment, [LXX]; ἅ. δυοῖν ποδοῖν, i.e. two square feet [Refs]four, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __2 with genitive, ἅ. τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ, ἔτεος ἑκάστου ἅ., [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __3 once on a time, formerly, ἅ. καὶ ἅ.[LXX]
  __II without any notion of number, after conditional and temporal Particles, if once, when once, εἴπερ ἐσπείσω γ᾽ ἅ. if once you have made a treaty, [Refs 5th c.BC+]: so with participle, ἐπὶ γᾶν ἅ. πεσὸν... αἷμα[NT+5th c.BC+] = sṃ (compare εἷς); -παξ akin to πήγνυμι.)
  ```
- `testament`: NT_only
- `causative_form_present`: 0
- `last_changed`: 2026-03-25T12:42:20
- `short_def_mounce`: once, once more, once for all; the Greek idiom "once and yet twice" means to do something repeatedly, "again and again" (<ref='Php.4.16'>Php 4:16</ref>)

  ```
  ἅπᾱς, ἅπᾱσα 
  (also ἅπανσα [Refs 5th c.BC+], ἅπαν (ἅ- = sṃ-, compare εἷς), strengthened for πᾶς, quite all, the whole, and in plural all together, frequently from [Refs 8th c.BC+]in all things, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __2 with adjective, ἀργύρεος δὲ ἔστιν ἅ. all silver, i.e. of massive silver, [Refs 8th c.BC+]; ἡ ἐναντία ἅπασα ὁδός the exactly contrary way, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __3 with abstract substantive, all possible, absolute, ἅπασ᾽ ἀνάγκη[Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __4 sometimes with Article, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  __II after [Refs 8th c.BC+] in singular, every one, neuter everything, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; οὐ πρὸς [τοῦ] ἅπαντος ἀνδρός not in the power of every man, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; ἐξἅπαντος εὖ λέγει in any cause whatever, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; ἅπαν γένοιτ᾽ ἂν ἤδη nihil non.., [Refs 5th c.BC+]: with substantive, ἅπαντι λόγῳ in every matter, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; τὸ ἅπαν, as adverb, altogether, [Refs 5th c.BC+]; καθ᾽ ἅπαν as a whole, [Refs 5th c.BC+]at all, [Refs 8th c.BC+]; but ᾰπᾱν [Refs 4th c.BC+]; ᾰπᾰν in anapaest meter, [Refs 5th c.BC+]
  ```
- `testament`: NT_only
- `causative_form_present`: 0
- `last_changed`: 2026-03-25T12:42:20
- `short_def_mounce`: all, every, whole
- `parsed_meaning_id`: 4974
- `delete_flagged`: 0
- `term_owner_type`: OWNER

  ```
  : stationary
  1) to be silent, be still, wait, be dumb, grow dumb
  1a) (Qal)
  1a1) to be silent
  1a2) to be still, die
  1a3) to be struck dumb
  1b) (Niphal) to be silenced, be made silent, destroyed
  1c) (Poel) to make quiet
  1d) (Hiphil) to make silent (cause to die)

**Row 26**
- `id`: 14798
- `parsed_meaning_id`: 4959
- `level_code`: 1
- `level_depth`: 1
- `sense_text`:

  ```
  patience, forbearance, internal and external control in a difficult circumstance, which control could exhibit itself by delaying an action 
  patience; patient enduring of evil, fortitude, Col. 1:11; Col. 3:12; 1Tim. 1:16; 1Pet. 3:20; slowness of avenging injuries, long-suffering, forbearance, clemency, Rom. 2:4; 9:22; 2Cor. 6:6; Gal. 5:22; Eph. 4:2; 2Tim. 4:2; Jas. 5:10; patient expectation, 2Tim. 3:10; Heb. 6:12; 2Pet. 3:15*
  ```
- `is_stem_label`: 0
- `sort_order`: 0
- `is_homonym`: 0

**Row 27**
- `id`: 14799
- `parsed_meaning_id`: 4960
- `level_code`: 1
- `level_depth`: 1
- `sense_text`:

  ```
  to have patience; to be patient; to exhibit internal and external control in a difficult circumstance, which control could exhibit itself by delaying an action 
  to be slow towards, be long-enduring; to exercise patience, be long-suffering, clement, or indulgent, to forbear, Mt. 18:26, 29; 1Cor. 13:4; 1Thess. 5:14; 2Pet. 3:9; to have patience, endure patiently, wait with patient expectation, Heb. 6:15; Jas. 5:7, 8; to bear long with entreaties for deliverance and avengement, Lk. 18:7*
  ```
- `is_stem_label`: 0
- `sort_order`: 0
- `is_homonym`: 0

- `analysis_note`:

  ```
  The hardship Israel endured on their journey is recalled as a shared ordeal — a burden of suffering experienced collectively and now recounted to Jethro, marking its weight on those who lived through it.
  ```
- `step_meaning_applied`: toil, hardship, distress, weariness
- `pole_is_metaphor`: 0
- `residue_flag`: 0
- `thing_type`: STATUS
- `triage_status`: ACCEPT

- `is_relevant`: 1
- `is_related`: 0

- `analysis_note`: Tribulation alongside bitterness describes the experiential hardship of being besieged and enveloped by God's discipline — the inner and outer weight of suffering pressing in from all sides.
- `step_meaning_applied`: toil, hardship, distress, weariness
- `pole_is_metaphor`: 0

- `is_related`: 1
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 39**
- `id`: 331756
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 29999
- `mti_term_id`: 32
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2 Pet 3:9, *makrothumeō* ("is patient/long-suffering") describes God's present, ongoing forbearance toward his people—a compound inner-life quality involving the long restraint of passionate reaction (makros + thumos). Predicated directly of God (attributed to God: yes) and rooted in his own volitional-affective character, this patience operates in the spirit and engages affect, volition, and moral-evaluation together. Its effect is explicitly stated: God's long-suffering produces an extended window for *metanoia* (repentance), so the forbearance is purposefully merciful, directed from God toward human beings (divine→human).
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:02:10.304Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:02:10.304Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 107**
- `id`: 454279
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30008
- `mti_term_id`: 33
- `cluster_code`: M33
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 9:22 makrothumia is God's "much patience" — "has endured with much patience vessels of wrath prepared for destruction." A status-noun for patience/long-suffering forbearance, here divine restraint: God bore (ferō) with much makrothumia — patience — the vessels of wrath, delaying the wrath (orgē) he willed (thelō) to show; so this term names the divine forbearance that long withholds the deserved wrath, even from vessels prepared for destruction.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.332Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.332Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 175**
- `id`: 417730
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30017
- `mti_term_id`: 33
- `cluster_code`: M33
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 2:4, *makrothumia* denotes God's patient, relational forbearance — his deliberate restraint of judgment attributed to God and directed toward sinners (divine-to-human). It engages moral-evaluation, conscience, and relational faculties in the recipient, since the proper response to divine patience is moral awareness and repentance rather than presumption. The term is grouped with *chrēstotēs* and *anochē* as part of a cumulative portrayal of God's gracious restraint. When the recipient is in the state of *agnoeō*, this forbearance is misread as divine indifference, producing presumption instead of the repentance it is designed to elicit.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T23:18:29.361Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T23:18:29.361Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 208**
- `id`: 356162
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30019
- `mti_term_id`: 33
- `cluster_code`: M33
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2Pe 3:15, *makrothumia* is predicated of the Lord—it is the Lord's patient forbearance—and readers are commanded to "count" it as salvation, meaning they are to reframe divine delay in judgment as a salvific act rather than a failure to act. The term is attributed to God and engages the affective and volitional faculties, reflecting the Lord's deliberate, controlled restraint of response across time. Its typological direction is divine-to-human: God's inner quality of long-suffering reaches toward humanity to create the relational space for people to be saved, and Peter's appeal equips believers to interpret their present moment through this lens.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T18:46:25.036Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T18:46:25.036Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 286**
- `id`: 438760
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30030
- `mti_term_id`: 61
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Heb 12:3 hupomenō is Christ's enduring — "Consider him who endured from sinners such hostility against himself." An action of steadfast endurance engaging volition, it is his patient bearing-up under the sinners' opposition (antilogia); the readers are bidden to consider this endurance precisely so that they "may not grow weary or fainthearted" in their souls (psuchē), so hupomenō is the model perseverance held up to sustain the faint.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T11:14:08.850Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T11:14:08.850Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 330**
- `id`: 404066
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30036
- `mti_term_id`: 62
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 5:3 *hupomonē* names the first inner fruit produced by *thlipsis*: "suffering produces endurance." As a quality of the inner person — seated in the soul and engaging volition, affect, and agency — it denotes the capacity to remain under pressure without giving way, a patient steadfastness that is actively willed and sustained. The verse presents it not as a natural human trait but as something *produced* (*katergazetai*) by the experience of suffering, indicating it is formed through process rather than possessed innately. As the first link in the chain (→ proven character → hope), *hupomonē* is both an end in itself and the means to further inner formation, equipping the believer to persist through affliction toward eschatological hope.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T22:15:40.928Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T22:15:40.928Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 393**
- `id`: 406094
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30044
- `mti_term_id`: 62
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 15:5, hupomonē denotes patient endurance as a quality so characteristic of God that he is called "the God of endurance." As a divine attribute, it is the basis from which God is petitioned to grant believers the ability to live in harmony; the origin is therefore bestowed-by-God flowing divine->human. The term engages affect and volition, since patient endurance involves sustaining a steady inner disposition under difficulty. Its relational implication is communal: the endurance that characterizes God is the very resource from which concord among believers is meant to flow, equipping them for harmonious life together in accord with Christ.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T22:24:32.221Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T22:24:32.221Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 426**
- `id`: 270789
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30046
- `mti_term_id`: 62
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2Cor 6:4, hupomonē denotes the active, steadfast endurance exercised in the face of afflictions (thlipsis), hardships (anankē), and calamities (stenochoria). It arises from within the person and engages both volitional resolve and the affective capacity to sustain suffering without collapse. Paul places it as the overarching frame ("by great endurance") under which all the following hardships are endured, making it the internal quality that transforms the entire list of external pressures into a demonstration of genuine apostolic ministry and credibility before God and others.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T11:24:19.169Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T11:24:19.169Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 549**
- `id`: 343127
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30068
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  *A.rakh* in Pro 19:11 denotes the lengthening or prolonging of *aph* (anger), forming the compound idiom "slow to anger." Good sense (*se.khel*) makes a person's anger long—that is, it produces in them a capacity to delay and restrain the affective reaction of anger. This volitional-affective capacity then enables the further act of overlooking (*a.var*) a *pe.sha* (transgression), which the verse calls the person's glory. The term thus bridges the inner cognitive quality of *se.khel* with its affective and relational fruit: patience that preserves relationships.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 1
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:50:36.245Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:50:36.245Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 572**
- `id`: 321788
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30072
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 6:2, a.rakh (vcid=30072) expresses the goal that "your days may be long"—prolonged life as the covenantal reward of fearing God and keeping his commandments. The term does not directly engage an inner faculty but names a tangible outcome bestowed by God on those who maintain reverence and obedience. Its typology is divine-to-human: the prolonging of days is a blessing that flows from God in response to covenantal faithfulness. Its relational implication is that sustained fear of God and obedience draw forth divine extension of life.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T15:11:29.134Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T15:11:29.134Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 595**
- `id`: 341575
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30079
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Pro 28:16, a.rakh denotes the prolonging or extension of life-days as the moral consequence that attaches to hating unjust gain. It does not engage an inner faculty directly but names the outward temporal effect that flows from the inward moral disposition (sa.ne) described in the same clause. The verse does not specify the agent who prolongs the days or locate this outcome in any constitutional organ, treating it as a wisdom-principle consequence. Its force is purely consequential, anchoring the moral reward of integrity in concrete, embodied life-extension.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:45:15.325Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:45:15.325Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 618**
- `id`: 317426
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30084
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Ecc 8:12, a.rakh describes the observable prolonging of the sinner's life ("prolongs his life"), serving as a temporal/empirical datum that creates the moral paradox the verse addresses. The term itself operates at the level of outward circumstance rather than inner life — no constitutional location, faculty, or produced inner effect is stated. It contributes to the verse's wisdom argument by naming the very anomaly — that wickedness does not immediately shorten life — against which the sage asserts his confident inner knowing (ya.da) about the God-fearer's ultimate well-being.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.287Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.287Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 641**
- `id`: 317472
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30085
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Ecc 8:13, a.rakh appears in negated form — the wicked "will not prolong his days like a shadow" — serving as the direct counterpoint to the sinner's apparent longevity in the preceding verse. The term operates at the level of observable temporal outcome rather than inner life; no constitutional location, inner faculty, or produced inner effect is stated. Its significance is structural within the wisdom argument: the failure to prolong is grounded causally in the wicked person's refusal to fear before God, making a.rakh the outward measure of the inner moral-relational stance denied.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.289Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.289Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 664**
- `id`: 340965
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30108
- `mti_term_id`: 84
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Pro 14:29, a.rekh combines with aph in the idiom "slow/long of anger" to describe the person who does not flare up quickly—whose emotional temper is patient and measured. This quality engages the affective and volitional faculties, signifying that the person exercises restraint over the rising of anger. The verse directly associates this quality with "great understanding" (te.vu.nah), establishing a causal or characterological link: slowness to anger is both a sign and a product of deep understanding. The contrasting qa.tser-aph exalts folly, placing a.rekh on the wisdom side of the polarity.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:41:05.942Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:41:05.942Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 687**
- `id`: 396629
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30113
- `mti_term_id`: 84
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  A.rekh in this confessional cluster describes God as "long" (patient, slow) with respect to anger, functioning idiomatically as "slow to anger" when paired with the implied "nostrils/face." It is a quality attributed exclusively to God here and belongs to the divine->human directional register—God's patient temperament orients him toward withholding wrath. This quality engages the affective and moral-evaluative dimensions of divine character. Jonah's prior knowledge of God's patience was integral to his prediction that God would not destroy Nineveh, and it forms a key element of the classical Hebrew divine-character formula he cites.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T21:41:50.622Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T21:41:50.622Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 710**
- `id`: 339873
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30127
- `mti_term_id`: 85
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Job 12:12, o.rekh carries the sense of temporal length—"length of days"—functioning as the creaturely condition under which understanding is said to reside. It is a status descriptor of duration rather than an inner faculty itself, yet it serves as the temporal vessel in which te.vu.nah dwells. The verse suggests that extended years produce an accumulation of understanding in the aged, making o.rekh the outer, temporal framework for an inner-life reality, though the verse does not specify an origin or constitutional location for that process.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:38:03.184Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:38:03.184Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 793**
- `id`: 437769
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30220
- `mti_term_id`: 130
- `cluster_code`: M18
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Zep 3:8 cha.khah is the waiting God commands — "Therefore wait for me, declares the LORD, for the day when I rise up." A directed expectant waiting engaging no inner faculty, it is the summons to await the LORD's appointed day; the faithful are to wait for God's rising to gather the nations and pour out his indignation (za.am), so cha.khah is the patient expectation of the day of judgment.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T11:04:13.046Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T11:04:13.046Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 847**
- `id`: 273511
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30227
- `mti_term_id`: 153
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Job 4:5, la.ah in the Qal stem conveys Job's state of weariness and impatience now that trouble has come upon him directly. This inner condition, received from outside through personal suffering, engages the affective and volitional faculties—a loss of endurance that manifests as restless grievance. Eliphaz pairs it with ba.hal (dismay) to paint a picture of total inner destabilization: Job, who once strengthened others, now exhibits both emotional agitation and volitional collapse when affliction reaches his own person.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T11:35:23.590Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T11:35:23.590Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 906**
- `id`: 382265
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30234
- `mti_term_id`: 153
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  la.ah in the Niphal describes Babylon's state of being wearied—or having wearied herself—through her multitude of counsels. The passive-reflexive Niphal nuance suggests that this exhaustion is self-induced: the very proliferation of advice-seeking has depleted her inner resources. Located in the soul and engaging both affect and volition, this weariness is the spiritual and psychological cost of trusting in human wisdom rather than the Lord, and it prefigures the futility of the astrologers and diviners who follow in the verse.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T20:39:13.953Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T20:39:13.953Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 934**
- `id`: 442468
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30235
- `mti_term_id`: 153
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Jer 6:11 la.ah is the prophet's weariness — "I am weary of holding it in." A status of being worn out/grown impatient engaging affect, it is Jeremiah's exhaustion at containing the divine wrath: full of the LORD's che.mah, he is la.ah — wearied — with holding it back, so he is commanded to pour it out on every age; so this term names the prophet's strain at restraining the wrath he is filled with, until it must be released.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T11:40:46.744Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T11:42:34.400Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 982**
- `id`: 329192
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30240
- `mti_term_id`: 2494
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Neh 9:32 te.la.ah denotes the concrete status of distress and hardship that has befallen Israel—kings, princes, priests, prophets, and fathers—from the Assyrian period to the present day. This suffered condition, received from outside through historical calamity, engages the communal affect and moral-evaluative consciousness as the community rehearses its plight before God. The term carries relational weight as a petition-ground: the community asks God not to let this accumulated distress seem insignificant in his eyes, making their suffering the relational claim upon divine attention.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T15:46:21.481Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T15:46:21.481Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1022**
- `id`: 389465
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30243
- `mti_term_id`: 2494
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  Te.la.ah denotes the accumulated hardship and distress Israel has experienced in the wilderness journey. It is received-from-outside — imposed by external circumstances — and engages the faculties of affect (the suffering felt) and perception (the awareness of that suffering). Constitutionally it touches both soul and body, as hardship of this kind is total in its impact. The relational implication is significant: Moses invokes the shared brotherhood of Israel and Edom as the reason Edom should acknowledge and respond compassionately to this recitation of suffering.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T21:09:35.404Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T21:09:35.404Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 69**
- `id`: 331756
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 29999
- `mti_term_id`: 32
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2 Pet 3:9, *makrothumeō* ("is patient/long-suffering") describes God's present, ongoing forbearance toward his people—a compound inner-life quality involving the long restraint of passionate reaction (makros + thumos). Predicated directly of God (attributed to God: yes) and rooted in his own volitional-affective character, this patience operates in the spirit and engages affect, volition, and moral-evaluation together. Its effect is explicitly stated: God's long-suffering produces an extended window for *metanoia* (repentance), so the forbearance is purposefully merciful, directed from God toward human beings (divine→human).
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:02:10.304Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:02:10.304Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 162**
- `id`: 356162
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30019
- `mti_term_id`: 33
- `cluster_code`: M33
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2Pe 3:15, *makrothumia* is predicated of the Lord—it is the Lord's patient forbearance—and readers are commanded to "count" it as salvation, meaning they are to reframe divine delay in judgment as a salvific act rather than a failure to act. The term is attributed to God and engages the affective and volitional faculties, reflecting the Lord's deliberate, controlled restraint of response across time. Its typological direction is divine-to-human: God's inner quality of long-suffering reaches toward humanity to create the relational space for people to be saved, and Peter's appeal equips believers to interpret their present moment through this lens.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T18:46:25.036Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T18:46:25.036Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 185**
- `id`: 417730
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30017
- `mti_term_id`: 33
- `cluster_code`: M33
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 2:4, *makrothumia* denotes God's patient, relational forbearance — his deliberate restraint of judgment attributed to God and directed toward sinners (divine-to-human). It engages moral-evaluation, conscience, and relational faculties in the recipient, since the proper response to divine patience is moral awareness and repentance rather than presumption. The term is grouped with *chrēstotēs* and *anochē* as part of a cumulative portrayal of God's gracious restraint. When the recipient is in the state of *agnoeō*, this forbearance is misread as divine indifference, producing presumption instead of the repentance it is designed to elicit.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T23:18:29.361Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T23:18:29.361Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 208**
- `id`: 454279
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30008
- `mti_term_id`: 33
- `cluster_code`: M33
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 9:22 makrothumia is God's "much patience" — "has endured with much patience vessels of wrath prepared for destruction." A status-noun for patience/long-suffering forbearance, here divine restraint: God bore (ferō) with much makrothumia — patience — the vessels of wrath, delaying the wrath (orgē) he willed (thelō) to show; so this term names the divine forbearance that long withholds the deserved wrath, even from vessels prepared for destruction.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.332Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.332Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 301**
- `id`: 438760
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30030
- `mti_term_id`: 61
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Heb 12:3 hupomenō is Christ's enduring — "Consider him who endured from sinners such hostility against himself." An action of steadfast endurance engaging volition, it is his patient bearing-up under the sinners' opposition (antilogia); the readers are bidden to consider this endurance precisely so that they "may not grow weary or fainthearted" in their souls (psuchē), so hupomenō is the model perseverance held up to sustain the faint.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T11:14:08.850Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T11:14:08.850Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 479**
- `id`: 270789
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30046
- `mti_term_id`: 62
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2Cor 6:4, hupomonē denotes the active, steadfast endurance exercised in the face of afflictions (thlipsis), hardships (anankē), and calamities (stenochoria). It arises from within the person and engages both volitional resolve and the affective capacity to sustain suffering without collapse. Paul places it as the overarching frame ("by great endurance") under which all the following hardships are endured, making it the internal quality that transforms the entire list of external pressures into a demonstration of genuine apostolic ministry and credibility before God and others.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T11:24:19.169Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T11:24:19.169Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 503**
- `id`: 404066
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30036
- `mti_term_id`: 62
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 5:3 *hupomonē* names the first inner fruit produced by *thlipsis*: "suffering produces endurance." As a quality of the inner person — seated in the soul and engaging volition, affect, and agency — it denotes the capacity to remain under pressure without giving way, a patient steadfastness that is actively willed and sustained. The verse presents it not as a natural human trait but as something *produced* (*katergazetai*) by the experience of suffering, indicating it is formed through process rather than possessed innately. As the first link in the chain (→ proven character → hope), *hupomonē* is both an end in itself and the means to further inner formation, equipping the believer to persist through affliction toward eschatological hope.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T22:15:40.928Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T22:15:40.928Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 526**
- `id`: 406094
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30044
- `mti_term_id`: 62
- `cluster_code`: M34
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Rom 15:5, hupomonē denotes patient endurance as a quality so characteristic of God that he is called "the God of endurance." As a divine attribute, it is the basis from which God is petitioned to grant believers the ability to live in harmony; the origin is therefore bestowed-by-God flowing divine->human. The term engages affect and volition, since patient endurance involves sustaining a steady inner disposition under difficulty. Its relational implication is communal: the endurance that characterizes God is the very resource from which concord among believers is meant to flow, equipping them for harmonious life together in accord with Christ.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T22:24:32.221Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T22:24:32.221Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 549**
- `id`: 292614
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 290
- `mti_term_id`: 79
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 9:19, *aph* designates the Lord's anger as an affective status attributed directly to God, directed toward Israel in a moment of relational rupture. It appears as part of a compound expression alongside *che.mah* (hot displeasure), intensifying the sense of divine wrath. The anger functions in the narrative as the motivating cause of Moses's fear (*ya.gor*) and intercession, indicating that the Lord's *aph* carried destructive force sufficient to annihilate the people. The relational implication is asymmetric: divine anger flows toward the human party, and Moses positions himself as mediator to absorb or deflect it.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T13:01:20.378Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T13:01:20.378Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 573**
- `id`: 340896
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 298
- `mti_term_id`: 79
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Pro 14:29, aph functions within the idiom qa.tser-aph ("short of anger/nose"), denoting the emotional state of anger that flares quickly in the impatient person. The term is body-located (the nose as the seat of hot breath/anger in Hebrew idiom) and engages the affective faculty. As a compound with qa.tser, it characterizes a person whose anger is brief in onset—i.e., hair-trigger—and the verse links this affective profile directly to the exaltation of folly (rum iv.ve.let). The contrasting figure, a.rekh-aph (slow to anger), possesses great te.vu.nah, showing that anger's management is a measure of understanding.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:41:05.940Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:41:05.940Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 596**
- `id`: 343104
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 300
- `mti_term_id`: 79
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Pro 19:11, *aph* (anger) appears in the compound expression *he'erich aph* ("makes long/slow his anger"), where *se.khel* is credited with enabling this affective restraint. The term identifies anger as an inner affective response that good sense moderates—slowing its onset so that the person is not quickly provoked. The relational implication is significant: restrained anger enables the person to overlook transgression (*pe.sha*), and this capacity is identified as the person's glory. *Aph* thus names the inner affective force that wisdom disciplines.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:50:36.245Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:50:36.245Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 619**
- `id`: 317426
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30084
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Ecc 8:12, a.rakh describes the observable prolonging of the sinner's life ("prolongs his life"), serving as a temporal/empirical datum that creates the moral paradox the verse addresses. The term itself operates at the level of outward circumstance rather than inner life — no constitutional location, faculty, or produced inner effect is stated. It contributes to the verse's wisdom argument by naming the very anomaly — that wickedness does not immediately shorten life — against which the sage asserts his confident inner knowing (ya.da) about the God-fearer's ultimate well-being.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.287Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.287Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 642**
- `id`: 317472
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30085
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Ecc 8:13, a.rakh appears in negated form — the wicked "will not prolong his days like a shadow" — serving as the direct counterpoint to the sinner's apparent longevity in the preceding verse. The term operates at the level of observable temporal outcome rather than inner life; no constitutional location, inner faculty, or produced inner effect is stated. Its significance is structural within the wisdom argument: the failure to prolong is grounded causally in the wicked person's refusal to fear before God, making a.rakh the outward measure of the inner moral-relational stance denied.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.289Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T14:52:08.289Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 665**
- `id`: 321788
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30072
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 6:2, a.rakh (vcid=30072) expresses the goal that "your days may be long"—prolonged life as the covenantal reward of fearing God and keeping his commandments. The term does not directly engage an inner faculty but names a tangible outcome bestowed by God on those who maintain reverence and obedience. Its typology is divine-to-human: the prolonging of days is a blessing that flows from God in response to covenantal faithfulness. Its relational implication is that sustained fear of God and obedience draw forth divine extension of life.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T15:11:29.134Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T15:11:29.134Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 688**
- `id`: 341575
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30079
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Pro 28:16, a.rakh denotes the prolonging or extension of life-days as the moral consequence that attaches to hating unjust gain. It does not engage an inner faculty directly but names the outward temporal effect that flows from the inward moral disposition (sa.ne) described in the same clause. The verse does not specify the agent who prolongs the days or locate this outcome in any constitutional organ, treating it as a wisdom-principle consequence. Its force is purely consequential, anchoring the moral reward of integrity in concrete, embodied life-extension.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:45:15.325Z

**Row 711**
- `id`: 343127
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30068
- `mti_term_id`: 83
- `cluster_code`: M25
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  *A.rakh* in Pro 19:11 denotes the lengthening or prolonging of *aph* (anger), forming the compound idiom "slow to anger." Good sense (*se.khel*) makes a person's anger long—that is, it produces in them a capacity to delay and restrain the affective reaction of anger. This volitional-affective capacity then enables the further act of overlooking (*a.var*) a *pe.sha* (transgression), which the verse calls the person's glory. The term thus bridges the inner cognitive quality of *se.khel* with its affective and relational fruit: patience that preserves relationships.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 1
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:50:36.245Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:50:36.245Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 734**
- `id`: 340965
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30108
- `mti_term_id`: 84
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Pro 14:29, a.rekh combines with aph in the idiom "slow/long of anger" to describe the person who does not flare up quickly—whose emotional temper is patient and measured. This quality engages the affective and volitional faculties, signifying that the person exercises restraint over the rising of anger. The verse directly associates this quality with "great understanding" (te.vu.nah), establishing a causal or characterological link: slowness to anger is both a sign and a product of deep understanding. The contrasting qa.tser-aph exalts folly, placing a.rekh on the wisdom side of the polarity.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning

**Row 757**
- `id`: 396629
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30113
- `mti_term_id`: 84
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  A.rekh in this confessional cluster describes God as "long" (patient, slow) with respect to anger, functioning idiomatically as "slow to anger" when paired with the implied "nostrils/face." It is a quality attributed exclusively to God here and belongs to the divine->human directional register—God's patient temperament orients him toward withholding wrath. This quality engages the affective and moral-evaluative dimensions of divine character. Jonah's prior knowledge of God's patience was integral to his prediction that God would not destroy Nineveh, and it forms a key element of the classical Hebrew divine-character formula he cites.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T21:41:50.622Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T21:41:50.622Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 758**

**Row 780**
- `id`: 339873
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30127
- `mti_term_id`: 85
- `cluster_code`: T2
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Job 12:12, o.rekh carries the sense of temporal length—"length of days"—functioning as the creaturely condition under which understanding is said to reside. It is a status descriptor of duration rather than an inner faculty itself, yet it serves as the temporal vessel in which te.vu.nah dwells. The verse suggests that extended years produce an accumulation of understanding in the aged, making o.rekh the outer, temporal framework for an inner-life reality, though the verse does not specify an origin or constitutional location for that process.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T17:38:03.184Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T17:38:03.184Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 863**
- `id`: 437769
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30220
- `mti_term_id`: 130
- `cluster_code`: M18
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Zep 3:8 cha.khah is the waiting God commands — "Therefore wait for me, declares the LORD, for the day when I rise up." A directed expectant waiting engaging no inner faculty, it is the summons to await the LORD's appointed day; the faithful are to wait for God's rising to gather the nations and pour out his indignation (za.am), so cha.khah is the patient expectation of the day of judgment.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T11:04:13.046Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T11:04:13.046Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 977**
- `id`: 273511
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30227
- `mti_term_id`: 153
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Job 4:5, la.ah in the Qal stem conveys Job's state of weariness and impatience now that trouble has come upon him directly. This inner condition, received from outside through personal suffering, engages the affective and volitional faculties—a loss of endurance that manifests as restless grievance. Eliphaz pairs it with ba.hal (dismay) to paint a picture of total inner destabilization: Job, who once strengthened others, now exhibits both emotional agitation and volitional collapse when affliction reaches his own person.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T11:35:23.590Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T11:35:23.590Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1001**
- `id`: 382265
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30234
- `mti_term_id`: 153
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  la.ah in the Niphal describes Babylon's state of being wearied—or having wearied herself—through her multitude of counsels. The passive-reflexive Niphal nuance suggests that this exhaustion is self-induced: the very proliferation of advice-seeking has depleted her inner resources. Located in the soul and engaging both affect and volition, this weariness is the spiritual and psychological cost of trusting in human wisdom rather than the Lord, and it prefigures the futility of the astrologers and diviners who follow in the verse.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T20:39:13.953Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T20:39:13.953Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1024**
- `id`: 442468
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30235
- `mti_term_id`: 153
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Jer 6:11 la.ah is the prophet's weariness — "I am weary of holding it in." A status of being worn out/grown impatient engaging affect, it is Jeremiah's exhaustion at containing the divine wrath: full of the LORD's che.mah, he is la.ah — wearied — with holding it back, so he is commanded to pour it out on every age; so this term names the prophet's strain at restraining the wrath he is filled with, until it must be released.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T11:40:46.744Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T11:42:34.400Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1117**
- `id`: 436108
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 1813
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Psa 79:5 a.naph is God's anger the psalmist questions — "How long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever?" Attributed to God (divine->human) and engaging affect and moral-evaluation, it is the divine wrath under which the community suffers; paired with the next line's "Will your jealousy (qin.ah) burn like fire?", the verse laments the seemingly endless duration of God's anger and jealousy, crying out for its limit.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T08:25:41.973Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T08:25:41.973Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1140**
- `id`: 448682
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64608
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 9:8 a.naph is the LORD's being "so angry" — "the LORD was so angry with you that he was ready to destroy you." A status of being angry (Hithpael, used always of God's anger), here at Horeb: even there, at the mountain, Israel provoked (qa.tsaph) the LORD, and he a.naph — was so angry — that he was ready to destroy them (only Moses' intercession averting it); so this term names the divine anger, kindled at the golden calf, that nearly consumed Israel at Sinai.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T14:09:28.454Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T14:09:28.454Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1163**
- `id`: 454440
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 1810
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 1Ki 11:9 a.naph is the LORD's being "angry" — "And the LORD was angry with Solomon, because his heart had turned away." A status of being angry (Hithpael, used of God), here at apostasy: the LORD a.naph — was angry — with Solomon because his heart (le.vav) turned from the God who had appeared to him twice; so this term names the divine anger at Solomon's idolatrous defection, despite his unique privileges.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.342Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.342Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1186**
- `id`: 454533
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64610
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 1Ki 8:46 a.naph is God's being "angry" — "you are angry with them and give them to an enemy." A status of being angry (Qal, of God), here anticipated: Solomon's prayer foresees that when Israel sins (cha.ta), God will a.naph — be angry — and give them to an enemy (o.yev) for captivity; so this term names the divine anger that, provoked by sin, would hand Israel over to exile — the prayer then pleading for mercy on repentance.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.357Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.357Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1209**
- `id`: 454556
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64612
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2Ch 6:36 a.naph is God's being "angry" — "you are angry with them and give them to an enemy." A status of being angry (Qal, of God), here in Solomon's prayer (the Chronicles parallel): when Israel sins, God will a.naph — be angry — and give them captive to an enemy in a land far or near; so this term names the divine anger at sin that would bring exile, the backdrop to the prayer's appeal for mercy when they repent.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning

**Row 1232**
- `id`: 454602
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64611
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In 2Ki 17:18 a.naph is the LORD's being "very angry" — "Therefore the LORD was very angry with Israel and removed them out of his sight." A status of being angry (Hithpael, of God), here at its height: provoked by the northern kingdom's persistent idolatry, the LORD a.naph — was very angry — and removed (sur) Israel from his sight, leaving only Judah; so this term names the great divine anger that culminated in the Assyrian exile of the ten tribes.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.363Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:20:20.363Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1255**
- `id`: 454625
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 1811
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 1:37 a.naph is the LORD's being "angry" with Moses — "Even with me the LORD was angry on your account and said, 'You also shall not go in there.'" A status of being angry (Hithpael, of God), here at Moses for the people's sake: the LORD a.naph — was angry — with Moses on account of Israel's rebellion, barring even him from entering the land; so this term names the divine anger that fell on the leader because of the people, excluding Moses from the inheritance.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.243Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.243Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1278**
- `id`: 454648
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 1812
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 4:21 a.naph is the LORD's being "angry" with Moses — "the LORD was angry with me because of you, and he swore that I should not cross the Jordan." A status of being angry (Hithpael, of God), here barring Moses: the LORD a.naph — was angry — with Moses on the people's account and swore he would not cross into the good (tov) land; so this term names the divine anger by which Moses, on Israel's behalf, was sworn out of the promised inheritance.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.251Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.251Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1301**
- `id`: 454694
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64609
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Deu 9:20 a.naph is the LORD's being "so angry" with Aaron — "And the LORD was so angry with Aaron that he was ready to destroy him." A status of being angry (Hithpael, of God), here at the calf: the LORD a.naph — was so angry — with Aaron (for his part in the golden calf) that he was ready to destroy him, until Moses prayed for him; so this term names the divine anger against Aaron that Moses' intercession averted.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.259Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.259Z

**Row 1324**
- `id`: 454717
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64613
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Ezr 9:14 a.naph is the wrath Ezra fears — "Would you not be angry with us until you consumed us, so that there should be no remnant?" A status of being angry (Qal, of God), here dreaded as total: Ezra confesses that to break God's commandments again by intermarrying with the peoples of the abominations (to.e.vah) would make God a.naph — be angry — until he consumed them utterly, leaving no remnant; so this term names the consuming divine wrath Ezra fears renewed sin would finally provoke.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.260Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.260Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1347**
- `id`: 454763
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 1815
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Isa 12:1 a.naph is the anger now "turned away" — "though you were angry with me, your anger turned away, that you might comfort me." A status of being angry (Qal, of God), here past and reversed: the redeemed give thanks because, though the LORD a.naph — was angry — with them, his anger has turned away so that he comforts (na.cham) them; so this term names the divine anger that, in the song of salvation, is graciously turned aside into comfort — wrath giving way to consolation.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.264Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.264Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1370**
- `id`: 454832
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64614
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Psa 2:12 a.naph is the Son's anger to be feared — "Kiss the Son, lest he be angry, and you perish in the way, for his wrath is quickly kindled." A status of being angry (Qal, here of the Son), it is the messianic warning: the rebellious kings are urged to kiss (submit to) the Son lest he a.naph — be angry — and they perish, for his wrath is quickly kindled; so this term names the swiftly-kindled anger of the enthroned Son, escape from which is found only in taking refuge (cha.sah) in him.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.269Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:24:06.269Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1393**
- `id`: 454878
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 64615
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Psa 60:1 a.naph is God's being "angry" — "O God, you have rejected us, broken our defenses; you have been angry; oh, restore us." A status of being angry (Qal, of God), here felt in defeat: the people lament that God has rejected them and a.naph — been angry — breaking their defenses, and they plead for restoration; so this term names the divine anger experienced as national defeat, the ground of the cry for God to turn and restore.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:26:14.361Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:26:14.361Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1416**
- `id`: 454901
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 1814
- `mti_term_id`: 1405
- `cluster_code`: M02
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Psa 85:5 a.naph is the anger the people question — "Will you be angry with us forever? Will you prolong your anger to all generations?" A status of being angry (Qal, of God), here lamented in its duration: the people ask whether the LORD will a.naph — be angry — forever and prolong his anger through all generations; so this term names the divine anger whose feared perpetuity the people plead God to end, seeking revival and steadfast love.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-10T16:26:14.362Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-10T16:26:14.362Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1464**
- `id`: 329192
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30240
- `mti_term_id`: 2494
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  In Neh 9:32 te.la.ah denotes the concrete status of distress and hardship that has befallen Israel—kings, princes, priests, prophets, and fathers—from the Assyrian period to the present day. This suffered condition, received from outside through historical calamity, engages the communal affect and moral-evaluative consciousness as the community rehearses its plight before God. The term carries relational weight as a petition-ground: the community asks God not to let this accumulated distress seem insignificant in his eyes, making their suffering the relational claim upon divine attention.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T15:46:21.481Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T15:46:21.481Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

**Row 1489**
- `id`: 389465
- `level`: VERSE
- `verse_context_id`: 30243
- `mti_term_id`: 2494
- `cluster_code`: M24
- `finding_value`:

  ```
  Te.la.ah denotes the accumulated hardship and distress Israel has experienced in the wilderness journey. It is received-from-outside — imposed by external circumstances — and engages the faculties of affect (the suffering felt) and perception (the awareness of that suffering). Constitutionally it touches both soul and body, as hardship of this kind is total in its impact. The relational implication is significant: Moses invokes the shared brotherhood of Israel and Edom as the reason Edom should acknowledge and respond compassionately to this recitation of suffering.
  ```
- `finding_status`: ANSWERED
- `provenance`: l2_meaning
- `flagged_for_review`: 0
- `created_at`: 2026-06-09T21:09:35.404Z
- `last_updated_date`: 2026-06-09T21:09:35.404Z
- `delete_flagged`: 0

## wa_cross_registry_links
_Rows: 4_

**Row 1**
- `id`: 76
- `file_id`: 25
- `linked_word`: anger
- `linked_registry_id`: 4
- `connection_type_id`: 1
- `connecting_term`: aph
- `note`:

  ```
  aph (H0639) is the primary anger term in the OT and is the constituent element of the compound arek aph (slow to anger). The patience word set necessarily includes the full anger term set because patience is defined in OT as the restraint or lengthening of anger.
  ```
- `last_changed`: 2026-03-16 11:32:38

**Row 2**
- `id`: 77
- `file_id`: 25
- `linked_word`: hope
- `linked_registry_id`: 78
- `connection_type_id`: 3
- `connecting_term`: ya.chal
- `note`: ya.chal (H3176) carries both the waiting and hoping senses and will likely overlap with any hope word set. The related word to.che.let (hope) is a derivative.
- `last_changed`: 2026-03-16 11:32:38

**Row 3**
- `id`: 78
- `file_id`: 25
- `linked_word`: humility
- `linked_registry_id`: 80
- `connection_type_id`: 4
- `connecting_term`: makrothumia
- `note`:

  ```
  Eph 4:2 appears in both the humility word set (tapeinofrosunē) and the patience word set (makrothumia): 'with all humility and gentleness, with patience, bearing with one another in love.' Col 3:12 similarly co-lists humility and patience.
  ```
- `last_changed`: 2026-03-16 11:32:38

**Row 4**
- `id`: 79
- `file_id`: 25
- `linked_word`: anger
- `linked_registry_id`: 4
- `connection_type_id`: 2
- `connecting_term`: la.ah
- `note`:

  ```
  la.ah (H3811) meaning 'to be weary, impatient' is the negative of patience and is semantically contrastive with the ARK root family. Jer 15:6 attributes la.ah to God ('I am weary of relenting'), creating a parallel with the god_as_subject instances in the ARK family.
  ```
- `last_changed`: 2026-03-16 11:32:38


## wa_session_b_dimensions

## wa_session_b_findings
_Rows: 1_

**Row 1**
- `id`: 83
- `finding_id`: DIM-116-001
- `registry_id`: 116
- `finding_type`: DIMENSION_REVIEW
- `finding`:

  ```
  Registry 116 (patience) shows a structural split: patience as human character quality (Character/Disposition, 7 groups) and patience as divine attribute (Theological/Divine-Human, 3 groups — 32-002, 33-002, 83-001). The divine patience groups show God's own inner disposition of forbearance modelled as the archetype of what believers are commanded to embody. Session B should examine whether biblical patience is explicitly presented as imitation of the divine disposition, and whether the vocabulary signals this connection.
  ```
- `raised_date`: 2026-04-07
- `session_b_instruction`: WA-DimensionReview-Instruction-v1.3-2026-04-07
- `delete_flag`: 0
- `thin_evidence`: 0
- `status`: pending
- `cluster_link`: M04,M17
- `cluster_link_basis`: translit
